name: Richard Holmes
abstract: Richard Arnold "Groove" Holmes  (May 2, 1931 – June 29, 1991) was an American jazz organist who performed in the hard bop and soul jazz genre. He is best known for his 1965 recording of "Misty".

concerts.label: Jazz '67: Julian "Cannonball" Adderley, Richard "Groove" Holmes, and Carmen McRae

about_quotes.speaker: Houston Person Part 1
about_quotes.evidence: I’ve worked with them all. ... Groove Holmes ... I’ve worked with a lot of organ players
about_quotes.doc_title: Houston Person Part 1 interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, Majesty of the Seas, May 29, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Maxine Sullivan
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Do you know Richard "Groove" Holmes? [INTERVIEWEE] No.

about_quotes.speaker: John Abercrombie
about_quotes.evidence: I mean Jack McDuff’s gone, Groove Holmes is gone, Johnny’s gone. And those were the places where young guitar players got a lot of that experience.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Abercrombie interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 19, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Doug Carn
about_quotes.evidence: I followed all the organ players, every last one of them. Groove Holmes, Jimmy Smith, McDuff, Doctor Lonnie Smith, Shirley Scott.
about_quotes.doc_title: Doug Carn interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, February 24, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Lou Donaldson
about_quotes.evidence: Then McDuff and Groove Holmes and Jimmy McGriff and all of them came in later.
about_quotes.doc_title: Lou Donaldson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: George Benson
about_quotes.evidence: Jimmy McGriff and Groove Holmes. These were all still our favorite guys.
about_quotes.doc_title: George Benson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
